full_response:
Step 1   Identify the indices that are being summed over (“fed”/“dummy”) and those that are not (“hungry”/“free”).

Left-hand side: T_{abc}\,A^{a}\,C^{bd}

• a occurs once up (in A^{a}) and once down (in T_{abc})  → summed over → dummy  
• b occurs once down (in T_{abc}) and once up (in C^{bd}) → summed over → dummy  
• c occurs once down (in T_{abc}) and once down again on the right–hand side D_{cd} → not summed → free  
• d occurs once up (in C^{bd}) and once down in D_{cd} → not summed → free

Right-hand side: D_{cd}

• carries the same free indices c and d.

Free indices: c , d  
Dummy (summed) indices: a , b

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2   Rewrite with different (dummy) index letters.

We may relabel dummy indices arbitrarily (but must leave free indices unchanged).  
Let a → p and b → q.  The equation becomes

T_{p q c}\,A^{p}\,C^{q d} \;=\; D_{c d}

This is algebraically identical because p and q are merely summation labels.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3   Count the number of independent component equations.

The tensor equation is valid for every allowed value of the free indices (c,d).  
In a four–dimensional spacetime each index can take the 4 values 0,1,2,3.

Number of component equations = (number of values of c) × (number of values of d)  
              = 4 × 4 = 16.

(For an n-dimensional space, the count would be n².)

\boxed{\text{Free indices: }c,d\qquad\text{Dummy indices: }a,b\qquad
T_{p q c} A^{p} C^{q d}=D_{c d}\qquad
\text{Number of component equations in 4D}=16} 

Confidence: 86%
